program: Travel English -- Europe Without a Translator
hub_subtitle: Travel English for the airport, the check-in counter, the hotel and passport control — so that Europe needs no translator

lesson.menu_title: One Day in Amsterdam -- The Plan and the Surprise
lesson.menu_desc: Will for the decisions you make on the spot, much and many for time and money, and a morning in Amsterdam that does not go to plan -- 38 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 8 -- Planning the Perfect Day
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rosângela | Lesson 8 -- Planning the Perfect Day

listenings.text: Good morning, and thank you for calling the Van Gogh Museum in Amsterdam. Please listen to this information before you come. First, our opening hours. We are open every day, from nine in the morning until six in the evening. On Friday we close at nine in the evening. Second, your ticket. We do not sell tickets at the door anymore. Every ticket is online, and every ticket has a time slot: the half hour when you come in. If you arrive at eleven, and your time slot is two, we cannot let you in. So please, look at your ticket. Third, today. Today is Saturday, and I am sorry: today is sold out. There are no tickets left for today, not one. But please listen, because there is good news. Tomorrow morning at nine we still have a lot of tickets. Nine in the morning is our off-peak time slot: there are not many people, the rooms are quiet, and the sunflowers are almost yours alone. At two in the afternoon this museum is very crowded, and you see a lot of heads and a little art. So come early. Fourth, the guided tour. The tour is one hour, it is nine euros, and there are only a few places on each tour, so book it together with your ticket. Is it worth it? Many of our visitors say yes: with the tour, you understand what you are looking at. And last: if you have a little free time before your slot, the garden and the shop are open to everybody, and they cost nothing. Thank you, and we hope to see you tomorrow morning.

Hello. My name is Saskia, and I am at the next table, with my coffee. Is this your first morning in Amsterdam? Can I tell you something? I am going to tell you the only thing I know about this city, and it is not in your guidebook. My husband and I come here twice a year. And for many years I had an itinerary. A real one, on paper, with a schedule for every hour. Nine o'clock, the museum. Eleven, the canal. One, lunch. Three, the market. It was beautiful, and it never worked. Never! Something is always sold out, or it rains, or there is a lot of traffic, and by eleven o'clock the whole day is broken, and I am angry, and my husband is very quiet. So now we do it differently. We plan two things. Only two! We are going to see one museum in the morning, and we are going to eat somewhere near the water in the evening. That is our itinerary. Two lines. And all the hours in the middle? Free time. That is where the day happens. Last October we had a few hours with no plan, and we walked past a little door with music behind it, and I said, oh, I will go in. Just like that. I decided it there, on the street, in one second. It was a small concert in a church, six euros, and it is the most beautiful thing I have ever done in this city. It was not in my schedule. It could not be. So here is my advice, from one traveler to another. Make a little plan, not a big one. Leave a lot of space in the middle. And when the day gives you something, say yes on the spot. It is worth it.

paras: It is eight in the morning in Amsterdam, and Rosângela is at the breakfast table with her husband. Outside the window there is a canal, a lot of bicycles, and a little rain. She has one full day, and a budget of sixty euros for the two of them. On the table, next to the coffee, there is her itinerary, and it has only three lines on it: the Van Gogh Museum in the morning, a canal boat in the afternoon, and dinner near the water. "We are going to take the tram at nine," she says to her husband. "The museum opens at nine. The opening hours are on my phone." Her husband does not speak English, and this morning he does not need to. He drinks his coffee, and he looks at her, and she is the one with the plan.

At nine o'clock they are at the museum, and there is a problem. The line is long, the entrance is crowded, and there is a sign on the door: SOLD OUT. Rosângela reads it twice. The old Rosângela, the one from Paris four years ago, looks for her son at exactly this moment. This one does not. She looks at the sign, she breathes, and she says it out loud, in English: "All right. I will ask." She walks to the desk. "Good morning. The sign says today is sold out. Is that for the whole day?" "I am afraid so. Saturday is our busiest day." "And tomorrow morning?" The man looks at his screen. "Tomorrow at nine we have a lot of tickets. Nine is off-peak: there are not many people, and the rooms are quiet." She does not think for very long. "Then I will take two for tomorrow at nine. What is the time slot?" "Nine to nine thirty. And there is a guided tour at the same time, if you would like it. There are only a few places left. One hour, nine euros." "Is it worth it?" "In my opinion, yes." "Then two, please."

Outside, her husband is waiting with the umbrella, and now the whole morning is empty: three hours of free time, and nothing in the itinerary. This is the moment she used to be afraid of. She is not afraid this morning. "Come," she says. "We have a lot of time and not many plans. I will find something." They walk along the canal with no schedule, and they find a market full of flowers, and a coffee shop with a table by the window, and a woman named Saskia who tells them where to eat tonight. At six o'clock, on the tram, her husband asks her in Portuguese what happened at the museum. And she tells him: the sign, the desk, the man, the tickets for tomorrow, the tour. It takes her two minutes. Four years ago, in Paris, that same conversation took her son, and twenty minutes, and a lot of embarrassment. This morning it cost her three words: I will ask.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Rosângela loses her day in Amsterdam, because the museum is sold out and there is nothing else to do.
The museum is sold out, and Rosângela solves it on the spot -- she asks, she books tomorrow, and she fills the empty morning with no plan and no help.
Rosângela's husband speaks to the man at the desk, because the sign on the door is in English.

items: Rosângela's itinerary for the day has only three lines on it.
Three: the Van Gogh Museum in the morning, a canal boat in the afternoon, and dinner near the water.
The museum is sold out, so Rosângela looks for her son to solve it.
The old Rosângela did that in Paris. This one says "All right. I will ask", and she walks to the desk herself.
The man at the desk says that nine in the morning is off-peak: not many people, and quiet rooms.
He says: "Tomorrow at nine we have a lot of tickets. Nine is off-peak: there are not many people, and the rooms are quiet."
Rosângela says no to the guided tour, because nine euros is too expensive for her budget.
She asks "Is it worth it?", the man says yes, and she takes two places on the tour.
When the morning is suddenly empty, Rosângela is afraid and wants to go back to the hotel.
She used to be afraid of exactly this. Not this morning: "We have a lot of time and not many plans. I will find something."

items.situation: You are at breakfast with your husband. Tell him the plan you made last night: the tram, at nine, to the museum.
tips: We are going to take the tram at nine.
We are going to see the Van Gogh Museum this morning.

items.situation: You arrive at the museum and the sign says SOLD OUT. Your husband looks at you. Say what you are going to do about it -- you decided it one second ago.
tips: Wait here. I will speak to the man at the desk.

items.situation: The man says tomorrow at nine is open. Decide, now, in front of him.
tips: Then I will take two for tomorrow at nine, please.
Perfect. I will book two tickets for the nine o'clock slot.

items.situation: You want to know if you have time for the boat before lunch. Ask about time -- careful: time is not countable.
tips: How much time do we have before lunch?
Do we have much time before the boat leaves?

items.situation: You want to know the number of museums your husband wants to see today. Ask -- museums you can count.
tips: How many museums do you want to visit today?
How many places are on the list for today?

items.situation: The morning is empty now: three hours, and nothing planned. Say it, using two quantifiers.
tips: We have a lot of time and not many plans.
We have a few hours of free time and no schedule.

items.situation: Your husband asks how much money is left for dinner. There is not much: fifteen euros. Say it.
tips: We only have a little money left -- fifteen euros.
There is not much left. Fifteen euros for the two of us.

items.situation: He asks if the guided tour, nine euros each, is a good idea. Answer with the expression of today.
tips: Yes -- in my opinion it is worth it.
It is nine euros, but it is worth it: with the tour you understand what you see.
